intro:
A character counter is a simple but essential tool for anyone who writes content with strict character limits. Social media platforms, SMS messages, meta descriptions, ad copy, and countless other contexts have character restrictions that you must stay within. Our free character counter shows you exactly how many characters you have used and how many remain.

Content creators and marketers rely on character counters daily. Twitter/X has a 280-character limit. Google search ads allow 30 characters for headlines. Meta descriptions should stay under 160 characters. SMS messages are 160 characters per segment. Knowing your character count prevents truncation and ensures your message displays correctly everywhere.

Beyond social media, developers use character counters to validate input lengths, writers use them to trim concise copy, and anyone filling out forms with character limits benefits from real-time counting. Our tool counts characters both with and without spaces.
howItWorks: Type or paste your text into the input area. The character counter instantly shows total characters (including spaces), characters without spaces, word count, line count, and how many characters remain if you set a custom limit. All counts update in real time.
interpretationGuide: Character count with spaces is what most platforms use for their limits. Characters without spaces is useful for understanding the density of your text. Setting a custom limit lets you see a progress bar and remaining count, making it easy to trim to fit.

faqs.question: What is the difference between character count and word count?
faqs.answer: Word count counts the number of words separated by spaces. Character count counts every individual letter, number, space, and punctuation mark. A single word like 'hello' is 1 word but 5 characters.

faqs.question: Does a space count as a character?
faqs.answer: Yes, spaces count as characters in most contexts, including Twitter, Google Ads, and SMS. Our counter shows both the total with spaces and the total without spaces so you can check either as needed.

faqs.question: How many characters is a Twitter/X post?
faqs.answer: Twitter/X allows 280 characters per post. URLs count as 23 characters regardless of actual length. Images, videos, and polls attached to tweets do not count toward the character limit.

faqs.question: How long should a meta description be?
faqs.answer: Google typically displays 155-160 characters for meta descriptions in search results. Keeping your meta description under 160 characters prevents it from being truncated in search results.

faqs.question: What is the character limit for Google Ads?
faqs.answer: Google responsive search ads allow 30 characters for headlines (up to 15 headlines) and 90 characters for descriptions (up to 4 descriptions). Ad extensions have their own limits.

faqs.question: How many characters is a standard SMS?
faqs.answer: A single SMS segment is 160 characters using GSM-7 encoding (standard English letters and symbols). Messages over 160 characters are split into multiple segments of 153 characters each, which may cost more.

faqs.question: What is the Instagram caption character limit?
faqs.answer: Instagram captions can be up to 2,200 characters, but only the first 125 characters appear before a More link. Front-load important content and calls to action in the first 125 characters.

faqs.question: Does emoji count as one character?
faqs.answer: Emojis typically count as 2 characters because they use Unicode code points outside the basic multilingual plane. Some platforms count them differently, so test carefully if emoji limits matter for your use case.

faqs.question: What is the LinkedIn post character limit?
faqs.answer: LinkedIn posts allow up to 3,000 characters. Articles have no limit. Comments allow up to 1,250 characters. The first 210 characters show before a See More link, so lead with your key message.

faqs.question: Why do different platforms have different character limits?
faqs.answer: Character limits are design decisions based on usability, database storage, and platform philosophy. Twitter's short limit encourages conciseness. Email has no limit. Understanding each platform's limit helps you write for each format.
